sections.content: I have 2 questions. My first question is, you had very strong accumulation and redemption trends this quarter, despite all the headwinds facing the Brazilian consumer. Do you expect these trends to continue in the back half of the year? Or are you beginning to see any softening?

sections.speaker: Unknown Executive
sections.content: Can you repeat the question, Catherine? We couldn't get the beginning of your question, if you will, please.

sections.speaker: Catherine O'Brien
sections.content: Sure. You had very strong accumulation and redemption trends this quarter, despite all of the headwinds facing the Brazilian consumer. Do you expect these trends to continue in the back half of the year? Or are you beginning to see any softening?

sections.speaker: Leonel  Andrade
sections.content: This is Leonel. Thank you for your question. Just to be sure, we had a very strong performance in accumulation and redemption, as you said. And your question is if we can maintain those same levels given the macroeconomic scenario in Brazil, yes. I think that we will be able to remain at those levels. Maybe we'll see lower, slightly lower growth. Right after the World Cup, we had good numbers in the second half of last year. So I think as of now, we do not see any indications in the terms of volume drop. And clearly, we are adding other options of in terms of redemptions. And those other options have substantially grown in the past 3 months and that includes the Connexions platform, which, as I said, complements our air arm. We have a big challenge with the high dollar right now. And so for international redemptions, we grew. Those were 3% and we are up to 20%, but they could be higher were it not for the macroeconomic scenario, which is very favorable right now. So we won't see such a strong growth, but I believe we remain, all in all, on the same levels. We don't see that levels -- those levels coming down. I hope I have answered your question.

sections.speaker: Gabriel Gusan
sections.content: The first question about international partnerships, if you have anything else in the pipelines after Copa and Korean? Or if you consider that those 2 companies are enough? Or if you have other partnerships on the horizon? And also, about the new marketing campaign, which would start after the 365 Reasons to Smile, if the next campaign will demand the same level of investments as we've seen so far?

sections.speaker: Leonel  Andrade
sections.content: Thank you, Gabriel, this is Leonel. Your first question, then, about international partnerships. We are discussing about other partnerships. We have just finished implementing other partnerships, and we have 2 or 3 opportunities which are under negotiations. So because of that, I'd rather not announce them now. But I believe that by the end of the year, we will have 1 or 2 new partnerships. But we're getting close to a reasonable level. We are now reaching 100 -- over 100 countries, so we are at a good level. It is very clear in the campaigns with the high income clients, we are also growing in that segment. The second question is concerning the marketing campaign. There is no anticipation of additional investments when compared to the current level of investments. The new campaign does not contemplate any additional significant investment. So I believe we'll remain in the same levels. In the second half of last year, we invested a bit stronger than now. So when we compare those 2 half, those 2 semesters, we don't see that number going up. I hope I have answered your question. You can come back if you want to.

sections.speaker: Catherine O'Brien
sections.content: The pace of your total other revenue growth outpaced the growth of your GOL management fee. I'm assuming that is tied to your co-bearing credit cards. If that is correct, was there a change to your card agreements this quarter? Or does the growth represent increase in the number of cardholders? And then, what do you see as the longer-term potential of these programs in terms of revenue generation or profitability?

sections.speaker: Leonel  Andrade
sections.content: Okay. Catherine's question, just to share with our colleagues here, has to do with the growth of other revenues. What would be the sources of those revenues? GOL credit cards? And what's our vision going forward? And how sustainable would that growth be when we consider the other sources of redemption for Smiles? So the growth of other revenues has a component which comes from the co-branded cards, but it also has an element which comes from the fees that we receive, especially from our retail partners when we use the Smiles Shop. So as we increase the penetration of the Smiles Shop and increased the penetration of the non-aerial redemptions and the total of our redemption levels, we should expect that those revenues will outpace the others because the comparison basis for the previous years is a very weak base, because we were still reactivating and relaunching the program in the previous quarter. So looking forward, we can expect that this revenue line will outpace the others because, as I said, we still have a low level of penetration in non-aerial redemptions. So the non-aerial redemptions, we expect them to grow. Today, they are at about 5% or 6% of the total redeemed miles. But in 1 or 2 years, we see a good opportunity to even double the amount of redemptions of the so-called non-aerial redemptions.

sections.speaker: Alexandre Spada
sections.content: How have clients accepted or welcomed this new product to redeem -- to use the miles to pay for the boarding fee? And also about the new categories of the Smiles Club. Are those numbers within the company's expectations? Are those numbers making a difference in the current results? Or you would say we're talking about very small numbers as of now, since those changes in the program are relatively new?

sections.speaker: Leonel  Andrade
sections.content: Thank you for your question. I -- well, the question about the redemption for the boarding fees, it's still too early to say. You can say it's better than we expected, but it's just too early, just too early to say. So late June, too early. About the Smiles Club, I'd rather not comment on it for now. We know we won't be alone in this industry for long. We know the competition is going to launch similar programs. But what I can tell you is that we are in line with plans, but we'll have new things to say very soon.

sections.speaker: Leonel  Andrade
sections.content:
Thank you all, again, for participating. On behalf of the company, I'd like to emphasize that we are very confident in our performance and in the opportunity we have ahead. Our ability to deliver is also very strong, and we remain 100% focused on operations. And we are now reaping the operating results we expected. I believe we will be able to maintain that level of performance in the new quarter, in the third quarter. The month of July was a very positive month. Our agreements are still holding, and our products are showing a very good performance. I'd like to thank our associates, emphasizing that we have 91 employees, all of them extremely engaged and all working hard to improve the company's performance. 
Once again, thank you. And I remain available, as our colleagues here on the team, for any additional clarifications. Thank you. Questions received through the webcast has been tackled by the participants or will be answered over e-mail by the IR team. Thank you all for participating, and we wish you all a good day.
